title: Osmosis — how water moves through cells
examTip: Osmosis is specifically about water — only water. Get the definition precise: partially permeable membrane, dilute to concentrated (or high water potential to low). Be ready to describe and analyse a potato strips experiment.
introTips: Osmosis is a special case of diffusion — it only applies to water molecules, and only through a partially permeable membrane.
You need to know what happens to plant cells and animal cells in different solutions, and use the correct vocabulary: turgid, plasmolysis, lysis, crenation.
The potato strips experiment is a classic required practical — you need to describe it, explain it, and calculate percentage change in mass.
intro: Your cells are constantly gaining or losing water — and it matters. If a cell takes on too much water it can burst. If it loses too much it shrivels. Your body controls this precisely. The process behind it is osmosis — a special type of diffusion that only involves water. Understanding osmosis explains why you feel thirsty after salty food, why plants wilt, and why a raw potato strip changes mass when you leave it in a salt solution.

keyPoints.heading: What osmosis is
keyPoints.content: Osmosis is the movement of water molecules from a dilute solution (high water concentration) to a concentrated solution (low water concentration) through a partially permeable membrane. It is a passive process — no energy is needed. The membrane allows water to pass through but not large solute molecules. Think of it as diffusion, but restricted to water only, across a specific type of membrane.
writeBullets: Osmosis = movement of water molecules from dilute to concentrated solution
Must be through a partially permeable membrane
Passive process — no energy (ATP) required
Water moves from where there is MORE water (dilute) to where there is LESS water (concentrated)
Only water molecules cross — solute molecules are too large

keyTerms.def: The movement of water molecules from a dilute solution to a concentrated solution through a partially permeable membrane — a passive process
A membrane with tiny holes that allow small molecules (like water) to pass through but block larger molecules (like glucose or starch)
A solution with a high proportion of water (low concentration of solute) — water moves AWAY from here in osmosis
A solution with a low proportion of water (high concentration of solute) — water moves TOWARDS here in osmosis

keyPoints.memTip: Remember: osmosis is diffusion's picky cousin — it only moves water, and only through a partially permeable membrane. The key phrase to write in the exam is 'from dilute to concentrated through a partially permeable membrane'. All three parts score marks. Write it five times.

keyPoints.heading: Turgid, plasmolysis, lysis and crenation
keyPoints.content: What happens to a cell depends on the solution it's in. Plant cells in pure water (dilute solution): water enters by osmosis → cell becomes turgid (firm and swollen). The cell wall prevents bursting. Plant cells in concentrated solution: water leaves the cell → cell becomes plasmolysed (the cytoplasm and membrane shrink away from the cell wall). Animal cells in pure water: water enters → cell swells and lyses (bursts) — no cell wall to stop it. Animal cells in concentrated solution: water leaves → cell shrinks and becomes crenated.
writeBullets: Plant cell in dilute solution → water enters → turgid (firm) — cell wall prevents bursting
Plant cell in concentrated solution → water leaves → plasmolysis (membrane pulls away from cell wall)
Animal cell in dilute solution → water enters → lysis (bursts — no cell wall)
Animal cell in concentrated solution → water leaves → crenation (shrivels)
Key difference: plant cells have a cell wall, so they don't burst in dilute solutions

keyTerms.def: Describes a plant cell that has taken in water by osmosis and is firm — the cell wall prevents bursting
When a plant cell loses water by osmosis in a concentrated solution — the cytoplasm and membrane shrink away from the cell wall
When an animal cell bursts because it has gained too much water by osmosis — animal cells have no cell wall to provide support
When an animal cell shrivels because it has lost water by osmosis in a concentrated solution

keyPoints.memTip: Four outcomes — two for plant, two for animal. For plants: dilute = Turgid (T for Tight and full), concentrated = Plasmolysed (P for Pulled away). For animals: dilute = Lysis (L for Lost its shape — burst), concentrated = Crenated (C for Crinkled and shrunken). T P L C — The Plant Lacks Cells.

keyPoints.heading: Water potential
keyPoints.content: Water potential is a measure of the tendency of water to move from one place to another. Pure water has the highest water potential (given a value of zero). Adding solute (making it more concentrated) lowers water potential — the value becomes more negative. Water always moves from an area of high water potential (dilute solution) to low water potential (concentrated solution). This is the same direction as osmosis — so water potential just gives osmosis a more precise, quantifiable language. You don't need to quote numbers at GCSE, but you must know the direction.
writeBullets: Water potential = tendency of water to move — pure water has highest water potential (= 0)
More concentrated solution → lower water potential (more negative value)
Water moves from HIGH water potential to LOW water potential
High water potential = dilute solution; low water potential = concentrated solution
This is equivalent to diffusion down a concentration gradient — just for water

keyTerms.def: A measure of the tendency of water to move by osmosis — pure water = highest water potential (0); dissolved solutes lower water potential

keyPoints.memTip: Water potential can sound complicated, but the rule is simple: water moves from HIGH to LOW water potential — just like diffusion moves from high to low concentration. They're the same direction. If you can remember the diffusion rule, you've already got water potential.

keyPoints.heading: The potato strips experiment
keyPoints.content: This is a required practical. Cut potato cylinders of equal size. Blot dry and measure initial mass. Place in solutions of different concentrations (e.g. 0%, 0.25%, 0.5%, 1.0% salt/sucrose solution). Leave for 20–30 minutes. Remove, blot dry, measure final mass. If the potato is in a dilute solution: gains mass (water enters by osmosis). If in a concentrated solution: loses mass (water leaves). In an isotonic solution (same concentration as the potato): no net change in mass. Calculate percentage change in mass using: ((final – initial) ÷ initial) × 100.
writeBullets: Method: equal potato cylinders, weigh, place in solutions of different concentrations, wait, blot dry, reweigh
Dilute solution → potato gains mass (water enters by osmosis)
Concentrated solution → potato loses mass (water leaves by osmosis)
Isotonic solution → no net mass change
% change in mass = ((final mass – initial mass) ÷ initial mass) × 100
Using % change (not absolute change) removes the effect of cylinders being slightly different sizes

keyTerms.def: Describes a solution with the same concentration as the cell contents — no net movement of water by osmosis
((final mass − initial mass) ÷ initial mass) × 100 — used in the potato strips experiment to compare cylinders fairly

keyPoints.memTip: The formula for percentage change trips people up under pressure. Write it as a fraction first: (change in mass / original mass) × 100. The 'change' is always final minus initial — if the answer is negative, the potato lost mass (water left). Practise with numbers: initial 4.0g, final 3.6g → change = –0.4 → –0.4/4.0 × 100 = –10%.

1.question: Which of the following is the correct definition of osmosis?
options: The movement of all particles from high to low concentration through any membrane
The movement of water molecules from a dilute solution to a concentrated solution through a partially permeable membrane
The active transport of water against a concentration gradient
The diffusion of dissolved solutes from concentrated to dilute solution
1.correctFeedback: Exactly — water molecules only, dilute to concentrated, through a partially permeable membrane. All three parts are correct.
1.wrongFeedback: Osmosis is specifically about water molecules, it's passive (not active transport), and it requires a partially permeable membrane. The direction is from dilute (lots of water) to concentrated (less water). Check the definition again.

2.question: A plant cell is placed in a concentrated salt solution. What will happen?
options: The cell will become turgid as water enters by osmosis
The cell will burst because water enters too quickly
The cell will become plasmolysed as water leaves by osmosis
Nothing — plant cells are unaffected by osmosis
2.correctFeedback: Correct — in a concentrated solution, water leaves the cell by osmosis. The membrane pulls away from the cell wall. That's plasmolysis.
2.wrongFeedback: In a concentrated solution, the solution has lower water potential than the cell contents — so water leaves the cell by osmosis. This causes the cytoplasm and membrane to shrink away from the cell wall: plasmolysis. Turgidity happens when water enters (in a dilute solution).

commonMistakes: Describing osmosis as the movement of 'particles' — it's specifically water molecules only. Using 'particles' or 'substances' will lose marks.
Forgetting to include 'partially permeable membrane' in the definition — it's essential and always worth a mark.
Saying a plant cell 'bursts' in pure water — plant cells don't burst because the cell wall provides support. Only animal cells lyse. Plant cells become turgid.
Confusing 'dilute' and 'concentrated' — in a dilute solution there is more water; in a concentrated solution there is less water. Water moves to where there's less of it.
Using absolute mass change instead of percentage change in the potato experiment — always use percentage change so different-sized cylinders can be fairly compared.
examTips: The definition of osmosis must include all three parts to guarantee full marks: (1) water molecules only, (2) dilute to concentrated (or high to low water potential), (3) through a partially permeable membrane.
In the potato experiment, always blot cylinders dry before weighing — water sitting on the surface would give an inaccurate reading. Examiners ask about this.
If asked to predict whether a cell gains or loses water, ask yourself: which side is more dilute? Water moves toward the more concentrated (less watery) side.
revisionCardBullets: Osmosis = movement of WATER MOLECULES from dilute to concentrated through a partially permeable membrane
High water potential = dilute; water moves from HIGH to LOW water potential
Plant cell in dilute solution → turgid (firm — cell wall prevents bursting)
Plant cell in concentrated solution → plasmolysis (membrane shrinks from cell wall)
Animal cell in dilute solution → lysis (bursts — no cell wall)
Animal cell in concentrated solution → crenation (shrivels)
Potato strips experiment: weigh before and after in different concentrations; calculate % change in mass
